narrative: As an Asian male applying to law school, your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 3, a Year 3 decile of 3, middle family income, an LSAT score of 32, an undergraduate GPA of 2.80, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 46.1% chance that you would fail it on your first try—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. This prediction stems from weighing factors like your academic rankings, test scores, and background against patterns seen in past students. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 3, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish level, signaling to the model a dip in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Next in importance, your middle family income actually worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing, as students from similar brackets have historically shown stronger bar outcomes than those from lower ones. Your LSAT score of 32, lower than the average of about 37, also nudged things positively but not enough to offset the top concern, since many with scores like yours still pass when other strengths align. Compared to typical students, your deciles are in the lower third while averages hover mid-pack, and your LSAT trails the norm, though full-time status matches the vast majority who succeed. While these core academics drove the decision, your solid background elements provided some counterbalance that kept the failure odds from being even higher. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male with clear drive—many in similar demographics overcome early hurdles—but the model flags risks based on data trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it toward 35 or higher, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted prep or additional coursework, and highlighting any upward trends or bar-specific study plans in a reapplication. Building a narrative around resilience, perhaps with letters from mentors who see your growth potential, could also help address these flags next time. With focused improvements, your profile could shift the odds decisively in your favor for future applications.
